skill.name: hipaa-compliance-auditor
skill.description: A clinical-grade PII/PHI detection and de-identification tool for healthcare text data using regex and NLP.
skill.summary: Detect and remove protected health information from clinical text with audit logging for HIPAA Safe Harbor compliance.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: Static analysis of 7 files (1335 lines) flagged 93 potential issues, of which 91 are false positives caused by markdown backtick formatting misidentified as shell execution, documentation URLs flagged as hardcoded network endpoints, and security checklist items incorrectly matched as path traversal or weak cryptography. The skill is a legitimate HIPAA compliance auditing tool with clean, well-structured Python code and no malicious intent, obfuscation, or data exfiltration. Two confirmed concerns: the CLI script writes files to user-controlled paths without path traversal validation, contrary to the security checklist documented in SKILL.md.

medium_findings.title: Unvalidated File Write and Read Paths in CLI Script
medium_findings.description: The script at scripts/main.py writes output files and audit logs to user-provided paths without path traversal validation. The --output and --audit-log CLI flags accept arbitrary paths including ../ sequences, allowing writes outside the intended directory. Similarly, the --input flag reads files from arbitrary paths without validation. This contradicts the security checklist in SKILL.md lines 228-231 which states path traversal should be prevented. Confidence: 0.90 - Direct evidence of open() calls with unsanitized user input from argparse, no os.path validation present.

low_findings.title: Security Checklist Not Implemented in Code
low_findings.description: SKILL.md documents a security checklist (lines 227-235) with unchecked items for path traversal prevention, input validation, and sandboxed execution. The Python script does not implement these protections despite them being listed as requirements. This creates a gap between documented security posture and actual implementation. Confidence: 0.85 - Documented checklist items are all unchecked (- [ ]), confirming none are implemented.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Controlled File Path in Write Operations
dangerous_patterns.description: The --output and --audit-log CLI parameters are passed directly to open() for writing without path sanitization. An attacker with control over command-line arguments could write files to arbitrary locations on the filesystem using path traversal sequences.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Controlled File Path in Read Operations
dangerous_patterns.description: The --input CLI parameter is passed directly to open() for reading without path sanitization. An attacker could read arbitrary files from the filesystem using path traversal sequences.

content.user_title: Audit Healthcare Text for HIPAA Compliance Automatically
content.value_statement: This skill detects and removes protected health information from clinical text using regex patterns and NLP entity recognition. It produces de-identified output with detailed audit logs so healthcare data can be shared safely for research and compliance purposes.
actual_capabilities: Detects all 18 HIPAA Safe Harbor identifier categories using regex patterns and optional spaCy NLP models
Replaces detected PII with sequential semantic tokens like [PATIENT_NAME_1] for consistent tracking across documents
Generates structured JSON audit logs with detection confidence scores, positions, and context for compliance documentation
Provides confidence scoring per detection to flag uncertain matches for mandatory human review before release
Validates de-identified output by re-scanning for remaining PII patterns and reports any warnings found
Supports both file input and direct text input via command-line interface with configurable confidence thresholds
limitations: Context-dependent PII such as rare disease names combined with geographic data may go undetected by pattern-based approaches
All de-identified output requires manual human review before release since the tool is an aid, not a replacement for compliance judgment
NLP-based name detection depends on optional spaCy model installation and will fall back to simpler title-based patterns if models are unavailable
Unstructured narrative text with embedded identifying details may contain PII not matching any predefined regex or entity patterns

use_cases.title: Clinical Research Data Sharing
use_cases.description: Researchers de-identify patient case reports and clinical notes before sharing datasets with external collaborators, preserving clinical meaning while removing identifiers.

use_cases.title: Healthcare Compliance Auditing
use_cases.description: Compliance officers verify that text documents and reports meet HIPAA Safe Harbor de-identification standards before release to third parties or publication.

use_cases.title: Medical AI Training Data Preparation
use_cases.description: Data engineers scrub protected health information from medical text corpora before using the data to train or fine-tune machine learning models.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Text De-identification
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the HIPAA compliance auditor to de-identify this clinical note: 'Patient Jane Doe, MRN 12345, was seen on 03/15/2024 for follow-up. Contact: 555-123-4567.'
prompt_templates.scenario: First-time user testing basic PII detection and removal on a short clinical note

prompt_templates.title: File-Based Batch Processing
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the HIPAA auditor on the file 'clinical_notes.txt' and save the de-identified output to 'clean_notes.txt' with a JSON audit log at 'audit.json'. Print statistics to confirm all PII categories were detected.
prompt_templates.scenario: Processing a file containing multiple clinical records for batch de-identification

prompt_templates.title: High-Sensitivity Detection with Custom Threshold
prompt_templates.prompt: De-identify this text with a confidence threshold of 0.85 and validate the output: 'Patient report for John Smith, DOB 01/15/1980, SSN 123-45-6789, address 456 Oak Ave, Springfield, IL 62701.' List any detections below the threshold that need manual review.
prompt_templates.scenario: Strict de-identification for data intended for public release or external sharing

prompt_templates.title: Audit Log Review and Compliance Sign-Off
prompt_templates.prompt: After de-identifying the input text, review the generated audit log to identify all low-confidence detections below 0.80. For each one, show the surrounding context and explain whether it is a true positive or false positive before approving the output for release.
prompt_templates.scenario: Compliance officer performing final quality assurance before approving de-identified data release

output_examples.input: Patient report for John Smith, DOB 01/15/1980, SSN 123-45-6789, address 456 Oak Ave, Springfield, IL 62701.
output_examples.output: Patient report for [PATIENT_NAME_1], DOB [DATE_1], SSN [SSN_1], address [ADDRESS_1].

output_examples.input: Dr. Williams contacted patient Mary Johnson at 555-123-4567 to discuss lab results from 03/10/2024.
output_examples.output: Dr. Williams contacted patient [PATIENT_NAME_1] at [PHONE_1] to discuss lab results from [DATE_1].

output_examples.input: Validation check requested on the de-identified output after processing a clinical note with 12 PII detections.
output: 2 detections below 0.80 confidence threshold need human verification.
Recommendation: Review these two items before approving output for release.

best_practices: Always run output validation after de-identification to catch any PII patterns that may have been missed during the first pass.
Set a higher confidence threshold of 0.85 or above when processing data intended for public release or external sharing to reduce risk of missed identifiers.
Review every low-confidence detection manually since these are the most likely to contain errors such as false positives or undetected PII variants.
anti_patterns: Do not rely on automated de-identification alone for HIPAA compliance without human review of both the cleaned output and the audit log.
Do not set the confidence threshold below 0.50 since this produces excessive false positives that obscure real PII and reduce review effectiveness.
Do not skip audit log generation when processing real patient data since the log is required documentation for compliance audits and traceability.

faq.question: What types of protected health information can this skill detect?
faq.answer: It detects all 18 HIPAA Safe Harbor identifier categories including names, dates, SSN, phone numbers, email addresses, medical record numbers, street addresses, IP addresses, URLs, and more using combined regex and NLP detection.

faq.question: Does this skill require an internet connection to work?
faq.answer: No, all processing happens locally on your machine. The skill uses offline regex patterns and optional local spaCy NLP models. No patient data is ever sent to external servers or APIs.

faq.question: Can this skill guarantee HIPAA compliance for my data?
faq.answer: No, this tool is an aid for the de-identification process, not a replacement for legal review. You must perform manual quality assurance on all output and consult legal counsel before certifying data as HIPAA compliant.

faq.question: What format does the de-identified output use?
faq.answer: Original identifiers are replaced with semantic tokens such as [PATIENT_NAME_1] or [PHONE_2]. Each token category is numbered sequentially so you can track what was removed and verify consistent replacement across the document.

faq.question: How accurate is the PII detection?
faq.answer: Detection confidence varies by identifier type. Structured data such as SSN and email addresses have high confidence around 0.90. Names and addresses have moderate confidence of 0.75 to 0.85 and are more likely to need manual review for false positives.

faq.question: Can I add my own regex patterns for organization-specific identifiers?
faq.answer: Yes, use the --custom-patterns flag to provide a JSON file with additional regex patterns. This is useful for detecting institution-specific identifiers such as internal patient ID formats or local medical record number conventions.
